---
title: "Notebook06"
solutions-release-date: "2026-09-16"
---

### Setup

Run all of the following before starting the notebook.

```{python}
#| tags: [noclear]
#| eval: false
! wget -q -nc https://raw.githubusercontent.com/taylor-arnold/fds2/refs/heads/main/funs.py
```

```{python}
#| tags: [noclear]
import polars as pl
from plotnine import ggplot, aes
from polars import col as c

import funs

ub = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/taylor-arnold/fds2/refs/heads/main/"
```

```{python}
#| tags: [remove]
#| include: false
ub = ""
```

```{python}
#| tags: [noclear]
storm = pl.read_csv(ub + "data/storms.csv")
```

### Questions

This notebook uses the record of North Atlantic tropical cyclones kept by the
U.S. National Hurricane Center. A row is not a storm. It is one observation of
one storm at one moment: where the center of the storm was (`lon` and `lat`,
in degrees), how fast its winds were blowing (`wind`, in knots), what
Saffir-Simpson `category` it had reached, and a two-letter `status` code, where
`TD` is a tropical depression, `TS` a tropical storm, and `HU` a hurricane. The
remaining status codes describe systems that are not quite tropical. Positions
are logged every six hours, so a storm that lasts a week leaves behind about
thirty rows. The columns `year`, `month`, `day`, and `hour` give the time of the
observation, and `doy` gives the day of the year, counting from 1 on the first
of January.

Unless a question says otherwise, just print the result of each block; do not
save it to a variable.

1. Make a scatter plot of all 25,112 observations with longitude on the x-axis
and latitude on the y-axis.

```{python}
(
    storm
    .pipe(ggplot, aes(c.lon, c.lat))
    .geom_point()
)
```

Nothing in that code knows what a map is, and yet the outline of one is there.
The points fan out from the tropics on the lower left, bunch up over the Gulf
and the Caribbean, and drift northeast into the open Atlantic, which is the path
storms take. The middle of the plot is a solid black mass, so this is the last
plot we can read this way.

2. There are too many points to follow any single storm. Plot the same two
columns again, but only for the 2005 season, which produced 688 observations
across 21 named storms.

```{python}
(
    storm
    .filter(c.year == 2005)
    .pipe(ggplot, aes(c.lon, c.lat))
    .geom_point()
)
```

3. One of those 2005 storms was Katrina. Filter down to the rows where `name` is
`"Katrina"` and plot the track. The result is not one storm. What happened?

```{python}
(
    storm
    .filter(c.name == "Katrina")
    .pipe(ggplot, aes(c.lon, c.lat))
    .geom_point()
)
```

**Answer**: The filter returns 70 rows describing three different storms. The
Hurricane Center recycles names on a six-year rotation, so a name is reused
until a storm is bad enough to have its name retired. There was a Katrina in
1981 and another in 1999 before the one in 2005. The name alone does not
identify a storm. The name and the year together do.

4. Fix the previous plot by filtering on the name and the year at once. You
should be left with 34 observations.

```{python}
(
    storm
    .filter((c.name == "Katrina") & (c.year == 2005))
    .pipe(ggplot, aes(c.lon, c.lat))
    .geom_point()
)
```

Now the shape of the storm's path is clear. Katrina formed near the Bahamas,
crossed the southern tip of Florida, turned northwest into the Gulf of Mexico,
and came ashore in Louisiana before running up into the Ohio valley.

5. The plot above shows where Katrina went but says nothing about how strong it
was. Using the same two filters, plot the day of the year on the x-axis and the
wind speed on the y-axis.

```{python}
(
    storm
    .filter((c.name == "Katrina") & (c.year == 2005))
    .pipe(ggplot, aes(c.doy, c.wind))
    .geom_point()
)
```

The whole life of the storm took nine days, from day 235 to day 243. It
strengthened slowly, crossed Florida, then doubled in strength in about
forty-eight hours over the warm water of the Gulf, peaking at 150 knots. The
collapse on the right of the plot is landfall. Each point is a separate reading
taken six hours after the last one, which is why they are evenly spaced. Drawing
a line between them is the obvious next thing to want, and it is the first thing
we will do next class.

6. Which observation recorded the highest wind speed in the whole dataset? Sort
the data by wind speed, largest first, keep the first five rows, and print the
name, year, status, category, and wind columns.

```{python}
(
    storm
    .sort(c.wind, descending=True)
    .head(5)
    .select(c.name, c.year, c.status, c.category, c.wind)
)
```

7. The record belongs to Hurricane Allen in 1980, at 165 knots, which is about
190 miles per hour. Plot its track.

```{python}
(
    storm
    .filter((c.name == "Allen") & (c.year == 1980))
    .pipe(ggplot, aes(c.lon, c.lat))
    .geom_point()
)
```

Allen ran almost the entire length of the basin, from the open Atlantic through
the Caribbean and into southern Texas.

8. Make a scatter plot with `category` on the x-axis and `wind` on the y-axis,
using every row of the data. What does this plot tell you about hurricanes?

```{python}
(
    storm
    .pipe(ggplot, aes(c.category, c.wind))
    .geom_point()
)
```

**Answer**: Nothing. The seven columns of points do not overlap at all, because
the category is not a second measurement of the storm. It is calculated from the
wind speed by looking up which band the speed falls into: 65 to 80 knots is a
category 1, 140 knots and above is a category 5, and anything under 35 knots is
recorded as a -1, meaning the system was not yet a tropical storm. A plot of two
columns usually tells you how two things relate to each other in the world. Here
it tells you the definition of a word. It is worth knowing which of your columns
were measured and which were derived from the others, because only the first
kind can surprise you.

9. Since the category is just the wind speed in disguise, it makes a convenient
filter. Plot the location of every observation that reached category 5.

```{python}
(
    storm
    .filter(c.category == 5)
    .pipe(ggplot, aes(c.lon, c.lat))
    .geom_point()
)
```

There are only 136 such observations in seventy years. They are spread across
the Caribbean, the Gulf, and the open Atlantic, but they all sit low on the
plot: not one of them is north of 30.3 degrees of latitude, which is roughly the
level of Jacksonville. Storms this size need very warm water underneath them,
and it runs out long before they reach the latitude of New York.

10. Now look at the season. Plot the day of the year against the wind speed for
every row in the dataset.

```{python}
(
    storm
    .pipe(ggplot, aes(c.doy, c.wind))
    .geom_point()
)
```

The outline of the hurricane season is the upper edge of this cloud. The record
starts in early May and runs to the end of December, but almost everything above
100 knots happens between day 220 and day 295, which is mid-August to late
October. Notice also that the points fall into rows rather than spreading out
smoothly. Wind speed is reported in five-knot steps, so thousands of
observations land on the same handful of values and sit exactly on top of one
another. Twenty-five thousand points went into this plot and you cannot tell
which parts of it are crowded. The tools for fixing that come later.

There is one other thing to see here: a small clump of points off on its own at
the far left, before day 20, cut off from the season by three empty months. Find
out what those are by filtering the data and taking one row per storm.

```{python}
(
    storm
    .filter(c.doy < 100)
    .unique(subset=["name", "year"])
    .select(c.name, c.year, c.month, c.day, c.wind, c.status)
)
```

Two storms, seventy years apart. Alice arrived on New Year's Day of 1955, and
Alex was a genuine January hurricane in 2016. A plot of 25,112 points led us to
the two strangest rows in the data, which is a good argument for plotting things
before you summarize them.

11. Do hurricanes weaken as they travel north? Take the observations from 1990
onward where the status was `"HU"`, add a column giving the wind speed in miles
per hour instead of knots (multiply by 1.15078), and plot latitude against that
new column.

```{python}
(
    storm
    .filter((c.status == "HU") & (c.year >= 1990))
    .with_columns(wind_mph = c.wind * 1.15078)
    .pipe(ggplot, aes(c.lat, c.wind_mph))
    .geom_point()
)
```

The answer is yes, but the shape is a ceiling rather than a slope. Weak
hurricanes turn up at every latitude, so the bottom of the plot stays flat. The
top does not. The strongest winds, up around 185 miles per hour, only ever
appear between about 12 and 30 degrees, and by 40 degrees nothing in the record
has passed 115. Try the plot once more with `c.wind` in place of `c.wind_mph`.
It is the same picture with different numbers on the axis, which is all that
changing a unit should ever do to a graphic.
